author: @osmbsmy.706
text:
I think this would make a fine dumbwaiter drive.  
If you approach the weight limit for the gear at a dangerous height, or your counterweight gives way completely, you still get a dampened fall rather than a full slip.
It has a nice redundant fail safe built in, at the gearbox, to prevent catastrophe and, of course, could be designed to handle a greater weight range relative to the counterweight.

author: @steve_jabz
text:
Would be really interested in seeing even some really basic torque tests with this.
Everyone else I've seen make them has had the slippage problem you pointed out at higher torque (and something else, I can't remember, I think it was cogging torque).
If this has actually solved that problem I'd be inspired to try some ideas myself. It would really be the perfect motor for 3D printers. People make high power 3D printed brushless motors but they're so dangerous and waste so much money on the way to get 1 working prototype

author: @kiwiwombatman
text: Great Idea, I imagine there must be force required to separate the magnets again , but this may be partly offset by the attraction of the next pair coming together .. is this force noticeable on your prototype ?

author: @rommelfcc
text:
Please attach a cycloid gearbox to a wind turbine, or wind collector, then using with a pulley system, more pully's, more travel and heavier weight can be lifted

Have it lifting the weight counter levering off a tall poll or poll's, that way all the mechanical mechanisms will be at ground level and can be anchored to a foundation... ❤

🤔 Could apply a brake on the cycloid gearbox so the the turbine can still make energy.

And disengage the brake when more electric load is required. Have no idea how to detect or do that, I'm not a sparky...

author: @mark.guitar
text: One plus one equals three! This is a rather good idea. What would you need to do to strengthen the shaft/wheel to give the whole thing more axle torque while keeping the simplicity of manufacture?

replies.author: @kadmow
replies.text:
- simply lengthen the magnetic contact area (torque transfer is limited)  and fully; bush and bearing all of the sliding surfaces (the eccentrics can have eccentric bushes with inner and outer needle bearings is one were to go crazy on the parts count, as the torque increase/speed reduction can be "crazy" per stage -   30:1 per stage could easily be derived by staggering magnetic contacts   - I made a 2 stage 100:1 model many years ago as a demo of functional uses of 3d printing  (I only used a single eccentric per stage as it was hand cranked)

PLA is not temperature tollerant, metal manufacture has its good points - plastic can make great bushing materials.

author: @goldcountryruss7035
text: I have been using these successfully for at least 40 years. the really nice thing is the reduction of a single stage ranges from 6:1 up to 86:1 and multiple stages are easy, so (86 x 86) up to 7,396:1 in 2 stages is possible. Before variable speed AC drives were commonly available, I also used a version that included a mechanical variable speed (SM Beier Variator + SM-Cyclo reducer). The result was a very strong drive in a small package. The cyclo speed reducers are capable of handling massive torque and high reduction ratios in remarkably small packages.

author: @josephpk4878
text: I'd imagine that this gear setup would have issues with vibration, when operated at higher RPMs. Perhaps, there's a way to minimize vibration - like using multiple "cycloids" that are thinner/lighter and arranged in a manner to offset the weight of the rest of the gears, though, there would still be a lot of shear force happening - just balanced with more gears. It would be interesting to see this setup with the cycloid gears completely filling up the chamber, with a "tooth" in every slot. If I only had more time on my hands... until then, I'll just observe and learn... while leaving the occasional comment. Thinking out loud, except in text format.

author: @crazyguysadvice
text: how to do the proximity of the magnets of eah other affect their lifespan of 'magnetitude'? What causes the permanent magnets to loose their strength over time Could you address the concepts in a video please?

replies.author: @trygvetveit4747
replies.text: It will last for billons of years, its one of the few things science knows/understand about magnetism and electomagnetism.

replies.author: @justtinkering6713
replies.text: ​@trygvetveit4747manufactured magnets can lose their power by heat, shock, alternating magnetic fields etc. With so many magnet moving in close quarters you have an already alternating magnetic field, so billions of years is a little incorrect.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: sure mate - but neodymium is a hard magnetic material it will last for centuries - i think it loses abut 1 percent every 100 years or so

replies.author: @justtinkering6713
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering I think I read 700 years depending on the environment it's in. I've totally destroyed one with a paper match.
Put one near an ac transformer and it won't last very long. I think samarium magnets are more robust.

replies.author: @crazyguysadvice
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I know this may sound strange but say one has lithium batteries right. What if in a sense one could use neodymium as a "store of value/energy" and it is only decoupled from the coil to prevent further energy generation, and in this sense can be seen as a "battery" in that unlike lithium it will tke ages to run out, especially if it is some type of stator "powering" a brushless generator.. I'm not sure if my intended meaning is being conveyed, my apology
